notes.anchor: match-ups of gladiators, adjournments of legal sureties, the Chrestus catalogue of pickpocketings
notes.note: Cicero mocks the triviality of the political gossip Caelius has been sending: gladiatorial pairings posted on the programme-boards, routine postponements of bail-bonds (vadimonia), and petty street-crimes recorded by some scribe called Chrestus. These were the Roman equivalent of tabloid news.

Cicero inserts a Greek word (politikoteron, 'more politically savvy') mid-sentence, a common affectation in his private letters to mark intellectual camaraderie; the Greek is transliterated in the Latin.

notes.anchor: what kind of edifice is going to rise
notes.note: Cicero uses the architectural metaphor of an 'edifice' (aedificium) for the constitution, implying that the current political situation is still structurally uncertain — will it stand or collapse?

notes.anchor: of which it is not possible to write, and not proper to write
notes.note: A standard letter-writing convention in ancient Rome: sensitive political discussions between trusted friends were reserved for face-to-face conversation, since couriers were unreliable and letters could be intercepted.

notes.anchor: no extension of time be granted on my province
notes.note: Roman provincial governors were assigned a fixed term; prorogatio (extension) was voted by the senate or people. Cicero, eager to return to Rome for political reasons, is urgently lobbying Caelius to block any such measure.
